title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Public Works Committee met on May 11, 2026 and approved several items including awarding a recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel for $126,900, adopting a resolution supporting state transportation funding, and directing staff to develop $100/day fines for illegal sewer discharges. The committee also approved construction agreements for the Military Road project and construction services for upcoming road projects.

agenda.item: Acknowledge April 2026 water and sewer permits
Review 2025 Water Utility PSC annual report
Water and sewer connection agreement for Radkey Avenue duplex

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a Local Road Improvement Grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Schofield Avenue and Community Center Drive, covering over 50% of the estimated $1.5 million project. This 2029 project will likely move to 2028. Michael noted the village has received over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years. Discussion also covered Weston Avenue settlement repairs by Integrity Grading and Excavating, and upcoming repairs on Schofield Avenue and Fuller Street for manhole and pavement issues.

Staff reported road limits were removed April 29th along with Marathon County. Spring activities included mailbox repairs, sign repairs, curb line sweeping, and hot mix patching. Flooding issues required dewatering operations near Weston Avenue for about a week. Two culverts failed on Jones Street requiring replacement. Discussion focused on developing a permanent pump solution for recurring flooding issues in the Crane Meadows area. Kennedy Park Farmers Market received positive feedback with no parking or traffic issues reported.

Josh reported on water service repairs, well maintenance, and significant clear water infiltration issues. April data showed 37 million gallons pumped into the system but 53-54 million gallons treated at the wastewater plant - approximately 15 million gallons of clear water infiltration. Staff identified residents illegally connecting sump pumps to sanitary sewers. An INI study with in-pipe technologies will help identify infiltration sources.

Michael reviewed the annual report showing net operating income of $482,911 for 2025, consistent with 2024's $490,000. Water loss is at 6%. Utility value increased to nearly $34 million with $6.7 million in infrastructure placed in service. The utility serves about 6,100 customers total - 5,250 in Weston, 830 in Rothschild. Cash reserves stand at $6.8 million. Motion to acknowledge passed unanimously.

Michael explained this League of Wisconsin Municipalities sample resolution supports long-term transportation funding solutions at the state level. The resolution addresses the gap between grant funding for one-time projects versus ongoing operational needs like street maintenance and snow plowing. Wisconsin remains relatively affordable for automobile ownership compared to neighboring states. Motion to recommend adoption passed unanimously.

Michael presented the recycled asphalt crushing contract, with King Gravel as the low bidder at $4.23 per ton for an estimated 30,000 tons ($126,900 total). Analysis showed the village's recycling program saves approximately $11 per cubic yard compared to City of Wausau costs, resulting in about $130,000 savings across three projects this year. Motion to award to King Gravel passed unanimously.

Committee discussed enforcement of illegal sewer discharges, particularly sump pump connections to sanitary sewers contributing to the 15 million gallon clear water issue. Current code allows fines of $100-$1,000 per day per violation. Committee directed staff to develop enforcement language starting at $100 per day, distinguishing between unknowing first offenses (warning with correction period) and willful violations. Motion passed unanimously.

Michael requested approval for a temporary long lateral service connection for a new duplex at 2230 Radkey Avenue until Radkey Avenue is reconstructed. The agreement requires the property owner to pay assessment when mains are extended. Committee added language requiring reconstruction no later than 2029. Motion to recommend approval with the date condition passed unanimously.

Michael presented the intergovernmental agreement with Village of Rothschild for water main relocation on Military Road. Steam Construction is the contractor. Village of Weston's share is $94,860 for sanitary sewer ($6,156) and water main ($88,704) work. The water main requires relocation due to storm sewer conflicts. Motion to recommend approval passed unanimously.

Michael presented proposals for construction inspection services. For the Alderson/Jelinek roundabout project, MSA was recommended at $37,315 (lower than Clark Dietz at $48,910). For Bladel Avenue, Clark Dietz was recommended at $73,730 despite being slightly higher than MSA ($70,400) because MSA proposed using a summer intern while Clark Dietz would use full-time experienced staff. Motion to approve both recommendations passed unanimously.

actionItems: Award 2026 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel at $4.23/ton (estimated $126,900)
Recommend Village Board adopt Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding
Direct staff to develop enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations at $100/day
Recommend Village Board approve Radkey Avenue temporary connection agreement with reconstruction deadline no later than 2029
Recommend Village Board approve Military Road construction agreement with Rothschild for $94,860
Approve MSA for Alderson/Jelinek roundabout construction services ($37,315)
Approve Clark Dietz for Bladel Avenue construction services ($73,730)
Special assessment meeting scheduled for May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM for Bladel Avenue project
Norcon to begin Schofield Avenue concrete repairs in eastbound lanes starting Wednesday/Thursday
Staff to provide analysis of cost impact from clear water infiltration at next meeting
Staff to review adding street lighting to Schofield Avenue from Birch Street to Camp Phillips Road in future CIP

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved a site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive, and adopted a new salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection areas. Both items passed unanimously with all members present voting in favor.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Site plan review for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive
Creation of salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
Planning and development department monthly project update report

discussions.item: Site plan review for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility
discussions.body: Staff presented the site plan for the new police department and municipal court at 3600 Community Center Drive. The project meets all B2 zoning district requirements. Police Chief Jeremy explained challenges with a gas pipeline requiring the building to be rotated and positioned on the higher part of the site to reduce fill costs. The building has capacity for 50-60 officers (current staffing is 40 officers and 4 clerical). An alternate storage shed was discussed, with commissioners suggesting it be placed further north to accommodate future building expansion. Motion was made to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees subject to staff conditions 2 through 5. Motion passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
discussions.body: Staff proposed a program with two annual inspections per facility - a warm weather inspection focusing on structural integrity and site drainage, and a winter operational inspection focusing on material handling and containment. A standardized inspection worksheet similar to DOT requirements was developed. Staff noted one existing facility near Sternberg that has never gone through the wellhead protection conditional use process and is closest to the village well. Commission agreed staff should reach out to that facility. Motion to approve the program with reporting back to both Plan Commission and Public Works Committee passed unanimously.
Motion to approve the April 13, 2026 meeting minutes as presented passed unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
discussions.body: Approved unanimously with no discussion.
Approved unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Planning and development department monthly update
discussions.body: Staff announced the first meeting of the joint Plan Commission/Village Board steering committee for the comprehensive plan update is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m. Staff will send calendar invites.

publicComment: Jim Pensel of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke via remote connection. He requested the commission urge staff to waive excessive landscaping requirements for the police department project similar to accommodations made for Greenheck Turner, to save taxpayer dollars. He also asked that the commission request an annual report on salt shed inspections to ensure follow-through, noting some zoning complaints are years old.
actionItems: Site plan for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court at 3600 Community Center Drive recommended for approval to Board of Trustees subject to staff conditions
Salt storage shed inspection program adopted for wellhead protection district with twice-annual inspections
Staff to report back to Plan Commission and Public Works Committee on salt shed inspection findings next year
Staff to reach out to existing salt storage facility near Sternberg that has not gone through wellhead protection conditional use process
Joint Plan Commission/Village Board steering committee meeting for comprehensive plan scheduled for June 1st at 5:00 p.m.
Electric vehicle charging station regulations to be discussed at future meeting in fall
Next regular meeting scheduled for Monday, June 8, 2026 at 6:00 p.m.

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an orientation meeting for new board members, featuring presentations from department heads on the Health Department, Social Services, Veterans Service Office, and Aging & Disability Resource Center. No substantive votes were taken beyond approving April 1st meeting minutes. The meeting provided educational overviews of each department's mission, services, and budget structure.

agenda.item: Call to order and remote attendance confirmation
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Operational functions required by statute (no items)
Review of standing committee ordinance, missions, duties, and responsibilities
Health Department presentation by Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka
Social Services Department overview by Administrator Leonard
Veterans Service Office presentation by VSO Aaron Galindo
Aging & Disability Resource Center presentation by Executive Director Mike Ray

discussions.body: Supervisor Cavelli moved to approve the minutes, seconded by Vice Chair Rosenberg. Motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

Administrator Lance Leonard explained how the committee oversees Health Department, Social Services, and Veterans Service Office as direct county departments, plus affiliated entities North Central Health Care and Aging & Disability Resource Center. He explained the committee's relationship to statutory boards including the Social Services Board, Veterans Service Commission, Board of Health, and Transportation Coordinating Committee.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented on the department's four divisions: environmental health (1,200+ licenses for restaurants, pools, campgrounds), nursing/communicable disease, family health, and community health improvement. She highlighted the nurse family partnership program graduating its first two-year-olds, a new homelessness system response position, and public health vending machines providing free resources including tick kits. The department has 37.6 FTE with roughly half its $5 million budget from tax levy.

Administrator Leonard presented on behalf of absent Director Christa, explaining the department's nearly 140 FTE covering child welfare, youth justice, children's long-term support, economic support, and child support. He highlighted the successful Family Keys housing stability pilot program partnering with the state, noting significant budget of $22 million with about $7 million from tax levy.

VSO Aaron Galindo reported the office increased from 2.75 to 3.0 FTE and had 8,149 interactions in 2025, up from 5,900. He noted $44 million annually flows to local veterans in benefits, with $2.6 million in retroactive payments in 2025 alone. Major initiatives include surviving spouse benefits outreach, housing stability partnerships with Center for Veterans Issues, and suicide prevention efforts after 7 of 30 county suicides in 2025 were veterans.

Executive Director Mike Ray explained the four-county regional model serving Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, and Wood counties with about 60 FTE plus 400 volunteers. Services include Meals on Wheels, Medicare counseling, caregiver support, and dementia care. He noted severe financial pressure as nutrition costs rose 40-45% over four years while federal funding remained flat, forcing meal and staffing reductions. County levy contribution is $413,135.

actionItems: Schedule North Central Healthcare for separate full presentation before budget process
New board members encouraged to email Chair Boots and Administrator Leonard with agenda item requests
Strategic plan discussion to be added to upcoming agendas
Next meeting corrected to Tuesday June 3rd at 3 PM (not May 29th as initially stated)

title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increases to park and recreation impact fees for new residential development, passed several ordinances including mobile food vendor licensing and e-bike regulations, and discussed ongoing challenges with the farmers market relocation to Kennedy Park. The board also adjourned the Board of Review to June 15, 2026.

agenda.item: Board of Review called to order and adjourned to June 15, 2026
Board of Trustees meeting called to order, Pledge of Allegiance, Roll Call
Public hearing on park and recreation impact fees (Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015)
Approval of minutes from previous meetings
Acknowledge reports from boards, committees, and commissions with chair updates
Consent agenda including vouchers and license renewals
Ordinances including mobile food vendor, nuisances, e-bikes, and transportation funding resolution
Discussion on farmers market location at Kennedy Park

discussions.item: Public hearing on park and recreation impact fees
discussions.body: Staff presented proposed increases to park and recreation impact fees from $300 per single family unit to $761, from $250 to $563 for duplex units, and from $250 to $495 for multifamily units, consistent with the 2022 public facilities needs assessment. Jim Pitel commented that the village lacks detailed data on actual park maintenance costs and is 'flying by the seat of our pants.' Trustee Lob Sra questioned when fees were last adjusted (2022). Ordinance 26-010 was approved unanimously (motion by Lope Sorrell, second by Zagami). Resolution 2026-015 was also approved unanimously (motion by Hermlang, second by Clark).

Chief Finkey explained that this ordinance establishes a reciprocal fire inspection agreement between Safer and multiple municipalities including Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, and others. A food truck inspected by any participating department receives a sticker valid across all jurisdictions, eliminating redundant inspections. Ordinance 26-008 passed unanimously (motion by Lope, second by Zagami).

The board debated amendments to the remote attendance policy. Trustees discussed removing language requiring health, safety, or welfare justifications for remote attendance, preferring a broader policy where members simply notify the chair and administrator 12 hours in advance. The amendment to strike restrictive language passed with two no votes from Trustees Hermlang and Lob Sra. The original motion as amended also passed with the same two dissenting votes.

The president appointed Stephanie Daniels and Golden Mua to the CLIPS committee. The motion was made by Hermlang, seconded by Lopes, and passed unanimously. Openings remain on Parks, Finance/HR (two openings), and CLIPS (one opening). The board agreed to include applicant resumes in future packets.

discussions.item: Farmers market location at Kennedy Park
discussions.body: Discussion-only item addressing concerns about the farmers market relocation from the former Camp Phillips/Schofield Avenue location to Kennedy Park. Trustee Beck raised safety concerns about parking on grass along Alderson. Renee (staff) explained the market grew from 25 stalls to 53 stalls and vendors now can keep their vehicles with them. The previous location was donated by a local business owner who is now developing the property. Mock Miller Park was discussed but rejected due to its remote location. No action was taken; the board acknowledged this is an ongoing evaluation.

The board approved a secondhand article and jewelry dealer license for Schofield Coin and Hobby LLC at 5703 Jamar Street, contingent upon satisfactory background check completion and receipt of occupancy permit. The business will buy and sell precious metals. Motion by Hermlang, second by Clark, amendment for contingencies passed unanimously, final approval passed unanimously.

Public Works Director Michael Dolan reported that an engineering study is underway regarding the speed limit on Weston Avenue. The 85th percentile speed was measured at 45 mph. The road is currently posted inconsistently with the village ordinance. The matter will return to the board, possibly at the joint meeting.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of 2052 Street spoke during public comment, criticizing the $108,000 expenditure for outside engineering consultants and questioning why the engineering technician position remains unfilled. He also criticized the process for appointing Lisa Beck to the Safer board, claiming she was introduced at a Fire Department meeting before official appointment. Pitel also complained about lack of detailed information on Kennedy Park in the meeting packet and called for greater administrator accountability.
actionItems: Park and recreation impact fees increased effective with Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015
Intergovernmental service agreement to be drafted for building inspection assistance to Town of Ringle
Comprehensive plan steering committee meeting scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 6:00 PM
Electronics drive date corrected to June 6th
Weston Avenue speed limit issue to return to board after additional speed study data collection
Applicant resumes for committee appointments to be included in future board packets
Staff to continue evaluating farmers market location at Kennedy Park
Remote meeting attendance policy updated with broader attendance provisions

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board held an adjourned organizational meeting where they approved committee appointments, elected Supervisors Hapa and Arstead to the Executive Committee, and unanimously passed a resolution reducing landfill tipping fees for disaster debris following the recent tornado and flooding that devastated Ringle and other communities. The board heard emotional public comment from affected residents requesting fee waivers.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Committee appointments discussion and remarks from Chair
Election of at-large Executive Committee members
Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs delivered remarks thanking Vice Chair Boots for assistance in developing appointments, emphasizing goals of increasing diversity of perspectives, developing new supervisors as leaders (noting 34% of the board is in first or second term), and respecting supervisor preferences. Motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Crims. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

discussions.item: Election of At-Large Executive Committee Members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg, who cited her advocacy for children and elders), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Supervisor Boots). Paper ballots were distributed and supervisors selected two names. Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the Executive Committee.
Items C10 through C14A were approved on motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Resolution 24-26: Out of Home Placement Reserve Increase
discussions.body: Motion by Supervisor Morash, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.
Motion by Supervisor P, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

Administrator Leonard and Solid Waste Director Hagen Busher presented the proposal responding to tornado and flood damage in Ringle and other areas. The state waived its $13.10 fee, Ringle waived its $2.33 fee, and the county proposed reducing rates to $36.94 per ton (covering costs only). Two residents, Jessica Toasty and Matt Haybeck, were granted special dispensation to speak, requesting the county waive fees entirely for 6 months. Supervisor Rosenberg and Supervisor Gao raised constituent concerns. The board first voted unanimously to suspend rules since the resolution hadn't gone through a standing committee. The resolution then passed unanimously on motion by Supervisor Arstead, seconded by Supervisor Morash.

publicComment: Two residents were granted special dispensation to speak on the landfill tipping fee resolution. Jessica Toasty requested the county waive fees entirely, noting the state, DNR, and Ringle were waiving their portions and that one business volunteer spent $40,000 on cleanup. She requested a 6-month waiver. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of the approximately 44 homes affected, noting many are still waiting for insurance adjusters and requested more time and relief for underinsured residents.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved for the new term
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to Executive Committee as at-large members
Resolution 26-26 approved reducing landfill tipping fees to $36.94/ton for certified disaster debris through June 17th
Administrator Leonard to continue discussions with Community Foundation regarding reimbursement mechanism for unreimbursed insurance costs
Solid Waste Management Board to develop clear policy within 6 months for handling similar disaster situations in the future
Supervisors encouraged to attend Wisconsin Counties Association officials workshops

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions and voted to forward a modified fee waiver resolution for tornado-affected properties to the full county board. The committee also received educational presentations from Solid Waste, Parks Recreation and Forestry, and Conservation Planning and Zoning departments.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public hearing: Tim Veland rezoning petition (Urban Residential/General Agriculture to Rural Residential, Town of Green Valley)
Public hearing: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture, Town of McMillan)
Consideration of fee waiver for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Educational presentation: Solid Waste Department overview
Educational presentation: Parks, Recreation and Forestry Department overview
Educational presentation: Conservation Planning and Zoning Department overview

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried with all in favor, no opposition.

discussions.item: Tim Veland rezoning petition (Town of Green Valley)
discussions.body: Land Use Specialist Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning from Urban Residential and General Agriculture to Rural Residential. The Town of Green Valley recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public testimony was offered for or against. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact and conclusions of law, seconded by Supervisor Kurt. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (Town of McMillan)
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the petition to rezone from Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture. Supervisor Leur asked about the conservancy nature of the property. Pogle explained there is a home and horse riding stable on the property, and the rezoning would make the structures conforming uses. The Town of McMillan recommended approval on April 20, 2026. No public testimony was offered. Supervisor Sefeld moved to approve, seconded by Supervisor Drew. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Fee waiver for April 17, 2026 severe weather impacted properties
discussions.body: Director Lori Mskimmons presented a proposal to waive county zoning and pouts fees for tornado-affected properties. Significant committee debate followed regarding fairness to other disaster victims, precedent-setting concerns, and financial implications. Supervisor Drew questioned where to draw the line for future events. Supervisor Drebeck proposed an amendment to limit the waiver to only reconnect fees ($160) for pouts systems, excluding other zoning and pouts fees. The amendment was seconded by Supervisor Kurt and passed. The amended resolution was then approved to forward to the county board, though the vote was not unanimous.

Director Dave Hagen Busher provided an overview of landfill operations, explaining the $13.10 state fee portion of tipping fees, renewable natural gas partnership with Vidi Energy, leachate treatment challenges due to PFAS regulations, and upcoming capital projects including $6 million cell construction and on-site leachate treatment facility.

County Forest Administrator Tom Lovelin presented on behalf of Director Jamie, covering the department's 44 full-time and 120 seasonal employees serving both county and city facilities. He noted Nine Mile County Forest generates $3.25 million annually from out-of-town visitors. Supervisor Sefeld acknowledged Tom's retirement effective June 1st after nearly 30 years of service.

actionItems: Forward amended fee waiver resolution (reconnect fees only) to full county board for April 17, 2026 tornado-affected properties
Forward Tim Veland rezoning petition approval to Marathon County Board of Supervisors
Forward Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition approval to Marathon County Board of Supervisors
Town of Green Valley should update comprehensive plan to reflect zoning change
Town of McMillan should update comprehensive plan to reflect zoning change

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee approved amendments to nuisance ordinances, recommended an e-bike ordinance with emoto restrictions, and authorized demolition of an abandoned structure. The committee also discussed overnight parking regulations and a church crosswalk request, deferring both items for further staff work.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
Mobile food vendors fire inspection amendment

discussions.body: Jim Senalt raised concerns about the new farmers market location at Kennedy Park, predicting grass destruction from vehicle parking. He questioned whether parking on grass violates village code and noted ADA handicap spaces are on grass rather than firm asphalt surfaces. He also questioned the origin of new fire inspection requirements for food trucks and criticized the age of some code enforcement cases, stating some have had no visible progress.

Code enforcement officer Audrey noted that enforcement-suspended cases may move back to pending as construction season begins. She announced 3020 Mountain View Avenue will be removed from the code enforcement roll after a property sale and new owner submitted repair permits. Police Chief Hunt reported 2,394 calls in March (77 per day), 410 traffic stops, and 281 citations. The department has hired its 40th officer, with officers 38-40 starting between now and January. Two SROs work with zoning during summer covering Weston, Rothschild, and Schofield. Motion by Sherwanka, seconded by Olsen, to acknowledge reports passed unanimously.

The committee reviewed final amendments including changes to tree limb height requirements (14 and 10 feet) that were switched as discussed in previous meetings, plus language requiring compliance with jurisdictional authority. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey, to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees passed unanimously.

The committee discussed a model e-bike ordinance for Chapter 82. Chief Hunt strongly recommended prohibiting emotos (electric dirt bikes) from roadways and sidewalks, citing safety concerns and comparing them to unlicensed dirt bikes. Discussion included whether electric scooters used by middle schoolers would be affected. The committee decided to leave officer discretion for kids' play scooters rather than explicitly exempting them. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Sherwanka, to recommend approval with the addition that emotos are prohibited from roadways, sidewalks, trails, or any public property passed unanimously.

Trustee Tolson presented research showing Weston's 12-hour no-parking window (4 AM to 4 PM) is far longer than surrounding municipalities, which typically use 4-5 hour windows overnight. Chief Hunt opposed eliminating overnight parking, stating it gives officers time to clear vehicles before plowing. DPW Director Michael indicated plowing rarely starts before 4-5 AM and suggested targeting 6-8 problem areas with specific signage instead. The committee decided to defer the item to July, with Michael to identify specific problem areas for targeted signage.

Mount Olive Church requested a crosswalk between their property and DC Everest administrative parking lot. A basic crosswalk would cost approximately $2,000-3,000; a lighted system would cost $25,000-30,000. Committee members expressed reluctance to set precedent of paying for requested crosswalks. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Sherwanka, to recommend board approval contingent on Mount Olive covering installation costs passed unanimously. DPW Director Michael will contact them.

Fire Chief presented a regional fire inspection program involving Wisconsin Rapids, Marshfield, Stevens Point, Plover, Rib Mountain, and Weston. Food trucks inspected by any participating department receive a sticker valid for all municipalities. Fire Marshal Marty already inspected one truck that failed due to improper LP piping (galvanized steel) and unsecured propane cylinders. Wausau opted not to participate, preferring their own system. Motion by Sherwanka, seconded by Campkey, to approve Ordinance 26-8 passed unanimously.

Staff presented three addressing errors: 5915 Delicoski Street needs to become 5915/5917 due to being an unknown duplex (homeowner agreed to change); Ross Avenue properties need renumbering due to sequence errors and a discovered apartment unit; and 2211 Radkey Avenue needs even-number correction. Chief Hunt noted an additional issue at Weston Villa Apartments on Delicoski where an 8-plex has 9 separate addresses. Motion passed unanimously to recommend approval to the Board.

Staff requested authorization to begin raze proceedings on an abandoned structure. The property has been abandoned since 2010-2011, was posted in 2021, and a planned fire department training burn was cancelled because the structure was too deteriorated for safe training. Chickens were previously housed inside. Current owners are up to date on taxes and prefer the village proceed with demolition; costs will be added to the tax roll. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey, passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt spoke regarding multiple agenda items. He criticized the new farmers market location at Kennedy Park, predicting grass destruction from vehicle parking and questioning whether this violates village parking-on-grass prohibitions. He noted ADA parking spaces are on grass rather than firm surfaces required by ADA. He questioned the origin of new fire inspection requirements for food trucks and criticized the age of some code enforcement cases, stating he can personally identify three with no visible progress.
actionItems: Chapter 50 nuisance amendments recommended for Board of Trustees approval
E-bike ordinance with emoto prohibition on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property recommended for Board approval
DPW Director to identify 6-8 specific problem areas for targeted no-parking signage and bring back to July meeting
DPW Director to contact Mount Olive Church regarding cost-sharing for Alderson Street crosswalk
Ordinance 26-8 for mobile food vendor fire inspections approved
Address corrections for E911 compliance recommended for Board approval
Raze order proceedings authorized for 4104 Shore Avenue
Staff to investigate property at Sternberg and South Timber with furniture and dumpster outside
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, July 7th at 5:00 PM

title: Tourism Commission
overview: Based on the published agenda, this special meeting of the Village of Weston Tourism Commission was scheduled to elect new officers and consider a $10,000 grant request for the Taste N Glow Balloon Fest. The meeting was set to address leadership positions for the commission and evaluate funding for a major annual event expected to draw 15,000 attendees to the area.

agenda.item: Election of Officers: Chair, Vice Chair, Secretary
Approval of minutes from the previous meeting: April 21, 2026
Grant Request: Taste N Glow Fest

discussions.body: The commission was scheduled to elect leadership positions including Chair, Vice Chair, and Secretary. Current commission members eligible for these positions include Ermeling, Frederick, Hodell, Jalowitz, and Zeyghami.

discussions.item: Grant Request: Taste N Glow Fest
discussions.body: The commission was set to consider a $10,000 room tax grant request from Taste N Glow Balloon Fest for their annual two-day event scheduled for July 10-11, 2026 in Marathon City. The event expects approximately 15,000 attendees and 45 estimated room nights, with funds to be used for advertising, potential shuttles, and lodging expenses for balloon pilots and crew members.

publicComment: Public comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Scheduled to vote on election of Chair, Vice Chair, and Secretary
Expected to consider approval of April 21, 2026 meeting minutes
Scheduled to vote on $10,000 grant request for Taste N Glow Balloon Fest

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Village of Weston Finance & Human Resource Committee was scheduled to meet on May 18, 2026 to review April financial reports including budget status and TIF district details, as well as receive a presentation on Clerk's Department operations and budget. This routine monthly meeting was set to provide fiscal oversight of village finances and departmental operations.

agenda.item: Approval of Minutes from Previous Finance & HR Meeting: April 21, 2026
TIF 1 and TIF 2 Detail Reports for April
Discussion on Clerk's Department Operations and Budget

discussions.body: The committee was scheduled to acknowledge the April 2026 financial report for all funds. According to the attached report, general fund revenue stood at 70.3% of budget while expenditures were at 40.7% of budget, with staff monitoring several line items that may exceed their budgets.

discussions.item: TIF 1 and TIF 2 Detail Reports for April
discussions.body: The committee was set to review the monthly Tax Increment Financing district reports for TIF 1 and TIF 2. These reports provide ongoing monitoring of the village's tax increment financing districts.
The committee was expected to acknowledge the legal details report for April, providing oversight of the village's legal expenditures.

discussions.item: Discussion on Clerk's Department Operations and Budget
discussions.body: The committee was scheduled to receive an educational presentation on Clerk's Department operations and budget. This follows a similar presentation format from the previous month when Planning & Development operations were reviewed.

publicComment: Public comments section was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Scheduled to vote on approval of April 21, 2026 meeting minutes
Expected to acknowledge April Budget Status Report
Expected to acknowledge TIF 1 and TIF 2 Detail Reports for April
Expected to acknowledge Legal Details for April

title: Parks Committee
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Parks and Recreation Committee was scheduled to meet on May 26, 2026 to receive updates on Kennedy Park and the Yellowbank Disc Golf Course, and to consider action on Dog Day at the Aquatic Center, graffiti on the Eau Claire River Trail, and kayak launch signage. The meeting included several community recreation facility matters relevant to Weston residents.

agenda.item: Approval of minutes from the previous meeting: April 27, 2026
Discussion and possible action Dog Day at the Aquatic Center
Discussion and possible action of Graffiti on the Eau Claire River Trail
Discussion and possible action Yellowbanks Kayak Launch Signs

discussions.body: Director Osterbrink was scheduled to provide an update on Kennedy Park developments. Based on previous meeting minutes, this was expected to include information on the capital campaign, grants, and ongoing renovation work.
The committee was set to receive an update on the Yellowbank Disc Golf Course. This was expected to cover current status and any developments related to the facility.

discussions.item: Dog Day at the Aquatic Center
discussions.body: The committee was scheduled to discuss and potentially take action on holding a Dog Day event at the Aquatic Center. This type of event typically allows dogs to swim at the pool at the end of the summer season.

discussions.item: Graffiti on the Eau Claire River Trail
discussions.body: The committee was expected to consider how to address graffiti issues on the Eau Claire River Trail. Discussion was scheduled to potentially include response measures or prevention strategies.
The committee was set to discuss and potentially approve signage for the Yellowbanks Kayak Launch. This follows previous discussions about the launch facility and its amenities.

publicComment: Public Comments and Written Comments were both listed as agenda items.
actionItems: Scheduled to vote on approval of April 27, 2026 meeting minutes
Expected to consider action on Dog Day at the Aquatic Center

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education's Committee of the Whole meeting on May 18, 2026 was scheduled to address multiple action items including the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, and policy revisions. The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

agenda.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling (Action Requested)
Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code (Action Requested)

discussions.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Thomas Jefferson Elementary as part of the Excellence in Action program. No additional details or presenter information were provided in the agenda.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in an estimated 5 minutes. Action was requested for this policy update.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on Employee Handbook Updates for 2026-27
Board was expected to vote on the Preliminary 2026-27 Budget recommendation
Board was expected to vote on 2026-2027 Paid Meal Price Update
Board was expected to vote on Activities & Athletics Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling revisions
Board was expected to vote on Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Riverview RFP Approval for HVAC and roofing project

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on approval of the Consent Agenda including appointments, separations, leaves of absence, retirements, meeting minutes, payment of bills, and donations
Action was requested for the 2026-27 Budget Reconciliation Plan
Board was expected to vote on 2026-27 Teacher Contract Approvals
Action was requested for Various Group Wage/Salary Increase of 2.63% for non-teaching staff
Board was expected to vote on WAMCS Charter School Contract Renewal for 2026-2031
Board was expected to potentially take action following closed session regarding non-renewal notices and contract evaluations

title: Water Works Commission Meeting
overview: The Wausau Water Works Commission approved the 2027-2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility, along with two sole source agreements for cross connection control services and polymer chemicals. The commission also received updates on lead service line replacements and wastewater projects.

agenda.item: Consideration of the minutes of the preceding meeting(s) - April 8, 2026 Regular Wausau Water Works Commission Minutes
Director's Reports - Update on Lead Service Line Replacements
Director's Reports - Wastewater Project Updates: Headworks Screening Project, Cherry Street Lift Station and Lift Station Assessment w/ Clark Dietz
Approving the 2027 through 2031 Capital Improvement Projects (CIP) Budget for the Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility
Approving the Sole Source Renewal Agreement for HydroCorp Non-Residential Cross Connection Control (CCC) Program
Approving the Sole Source Request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA Polymers used to thicken solids in the Belt Filter Presses and Gravity Belt Thickeners
Discussion regarding the Wastewater - Final WPDES Permit Issued with a permit term of five years

discussions.body: The commission approved the minutes from the April 8, 2026 regular meeting. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Update on Lead Service Line Replacements
discussions.body: The director provided an update on lead service line replacements. This was an informational report with no action required.

The director reported on wastewater projects including the Headworks Screening Project, Cherry Street Lift Station, and Lift Station Assessment with Clark Dietz. This was an informational report with no action required.
The commission approved the 2027 through 2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for the Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Sole Source Renewal Agreement for HydroCorp Cross Connection Control Program
discussions.body: The commission approved the sole source renewal agreement for HydroCorp Non-Residential Cross Connection Control Program. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Sole Source Request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA Polymers
discussions.body: The commission approved the sole source request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA polymers used to thicken solids in the Belt Filter Presses and Gravity Belt Thickeners. The motion passed.
The commission discussed the final WPDES permit issued with a five-year term. This was a discussion item with no formal action taken.

actionItems: Implement 2027-2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility
Execute sole source renewal agreement with HydroCorp for Non-Residential Cross Connection Control Program
Proceed with sole source procurement of MCEP9 and K144L-NA polymers for wastewater treatment
Next meeting scheduled for June 2, 2026 at 11:00 AM

title: Economic Development Committee Meeting
overview: The Economic Development Committee held its organizational meeting for the 2026-2028 term, with elections for chairperson and vice chairperson and establishment of a regular meeting schedule. The committee also considered deed restriction changes for 725 S. 84th Avenue and received an overview of the Community Development Department.

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this special meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to convene in closed session to hold a pupil expulsion hearing. The board was expected to deliberate privately and potentially take action regarding the student expulsion matter, with the possibility of reconvening in open session for further action if necessary.

agenda.item: Closed Session - Pupil Expulsion Hearing pursuant to s. 19.85(1)(a), (f), and (g), and s. 118.125 of Wisconsin Statutes

discussions.body: The Board of Education was scheduled to convene in closed session under Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(a), (f), and (g), as well as section 118.125, to conduct a pupil expulsion hearing. The board was expected to deliberate privately at the conclusion of the hearing and potentially take action in closed session if necessary and appropriate. Following the closed session, the board was scheduled to potentially reconvene into open session for further action.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Week of the Young Child Proclamation
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.
actionItems: Bike Rack Request Form approved for implementation by Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee
Special assessments levied for 2025 Street Construction Projects
